content:
# Numbers Chapter 35 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 193-194 | **Source Image:** `NUM_035_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0141-pa_0142

---

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses on the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying,

**2** "Command the sons of Israel to give to the Levites, from the inheritance they possess, cities in which to dwell. And you shall give to the Levites pasturelands surrounding the cities.

**3** And the cities shall be theirs to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their cattle and for their livestock and for all their animals.

**4** And the pasturelands of the cities that you give to the Levites shall extend from the wall of the city outward one thousand cubits all around.

**5** And you shall measure outside the city — on the eastern side two thousand cubits, and on the southern side two thousand cubits, and on the western side two thousand cubits, and on the northern side two thousand cubits — with the city at the center. This shall be for them the pasturelands of the cities.

**6** And among the cities that you give to the Levites shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall designate for the one who kills someone to flee there. And in addition to them you shall give forty-two cities.

**7** All the cities that you give to the Levites shall be forty-eight cities — them and their pasturelands.

**8** And the cities that you give from the possession of the sons of Israel — from the larger tribe you shall take more, and from the smaller tribe you shall take fewer. Each tribe shall give from its cities to the Levites in proportion to the inheritance it receives."

**9** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**10** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan,

**11** you shall select cities for yourselves — cities of refuge they shall be for you — so that one who kills a person unintentionally may flee there.

**12** And the cities shall serve you as refuge from the blood-avenger, so that the one who kills shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment.

**13** And the cities that you give — six cities of refuge they shall be for you.

**14** Three of the cities you shall give on the other side of the Jordan, and three of the cities you shall give in the land of Canaan. Cities of refuge they shall be.

**15** For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident alien among them, these six cities shall be for refuge, so that anyone who kills a person unintentionally may flee there.

**16** But if he strikes him with an instrument of iron and he dies — he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.

**17** And if he strikes him with a stone in hand large enough to kill, and he dies — he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.

**18** Or if he strikes him with a wooden weapon in hand large enough to kill, and he dies — he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.

**19** The blood-avenger himself shall put the murderer to death. When he encounters him, he shall put him to death.

**20** And if out of hatred he pushes him, or hurls something at him with malicious intent and he dies,

**21** or in enmity strikes him with his hand and he dies — the one who struck him shall surely be put to death; he is a murderer. The blood-avenger shall put the murderer to death when he encounters him.

**22** But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushes him, or throws any object at him without malicious intent,

**23** or with any stone large enough to kill, without seeing him, and it falls upon him and he dies — and he was not his enemy, and he did not seek his harm —

**24** then the congregation shall judge between the one who struck and the blood-avenger according to these ordinances.

**25** And the congregation shall rescue the one who killed from the hand of the blood-avenger, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge where he had fled. And he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil.

**26** But if the one who killed ever goes beyond the boundary of his city of refuge where he fled,

**27** and the blood-avenger finds him outside the boundary of his city of refuge, and the blood-avenger kills the one who killed — there is no bloodguilt upon him.

**28** For in his city of refuge he must dwell until the death of the high priest. And after the death of the high priest, the one who killed may return to the land of his possession.

**29** And these things shall be a statute of judgment for you throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**30** Anyone who kills a person — on the testimony of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death. But a single witness shall not testify against a person to put him to death.

**31** And you shall accept no ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty and deserves to die; he shall surely be put to death.

**32** And you shall accept no ransom for one who has fled to his city of refuge, allowing him to return to dwell in the land before the death of the high priest.

**33** And you shall not pollute the land in which you live, for blood — it pollutes the land. And no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it.

**34** And you shall not defile the land in which you dwell, in the midst of which I dwell, for I am YHWH, dwelling in the midst of the sons of Israel."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-8 — Levitical Cities and Pasturelands:**
The Hebrew מִגְרָשׁ (*migrash*) denotes open land surrounding a city used for grazing and general livelihood, not merely "suburbs" as some older translations render it. The Levites, having received no tribal land allotment, are instead distributed throughout all Israel — a theological statement that the priestly tribe is woven into the fabric of the entire nation. The measurement system (one thousand cubits from the wall, two thousand cubits measured outward on each side) has generated extensive rabbinic discussion; the most coherent reading treats verse 4 as the pastureland strip and verse 5 as a broader usage zone. The Vaticanus text (LXX) closely parallels the MT here, using πόλεις (poleis) and the cognate terminology for pastureland (τὰ προάστεια / ta proasteia).

**Verse 6 — Cities of Refuge (עָרֵי הַמִּקְלָט):**
The Hebrew מִקְלָט (*miqlat*) derives from the root קלט, meaning "to receive, to absorb." These cities literally "absorb" the fugitive. This is not mere asylum — it is a divinely structured mechanism to prevent cycles of retaliatory violence while still honoring the gravity of human life taken. The institution stands as a remarkable balance between justice and mercy in ancient law.

**Verses 12, 19, 21, 24-25 — The Blood-Avenger (גֹּאֵל הַדָּם):**
The גֹּאֵל (*go'el*) is the kinsman-redeemer, the near relative whose duty extends across property redemption, family honor, and here, blood justice. The term carries deep covenantal weight — this same word describes YHWH's redemptive action toward Israel. The blood-avenger is not a vigilante but an authorized agent of communal justice operating within defined legal boundaries. The congregation (עֵדָה, *'edah*) serves as the judicial body that determines intent.

**Verses 16-18 — Instruments of Death:**
The law carefully distinguishes murder by the weapon used: iron, stone, wood — each "in hand" (בְּיָד, *b'yad*), emphasizing the deliberate wielding. The phrase כְּלִי בַרְזֶל (*kli barzel*, "instrument of iron") suggests a crafted weapon. The repeated formula "he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death" (מוֹת יוּמַת, *mot yumat*) carries the weight of absolute legal certainty. The Vaticanus text maintains the same tripartite structure.

**Verses 20-23 — Intent as the Decisive Factor:**
The Hebrew text draws a sharp line between בְּשִׂנְאָה (*b'sin'ah*, "in hatred") and בְּפֶתַע בְּלֹא־אֵיבָה (*b'feta' b'lo-'eivah*, "suddenly without enmity"). This is one of the earliest legal codifications distinguishing premeditated murder from involuntary manslaughter based on the inner disposition of the killer. The terms צְדִיָּה (*ts'diyyah*, "malicious intent/lying in wait") in verse 20 and בְּלֹא צְדִיָּה ("without malicious intent") in verse 22 form a precise legal antithesis.

**Verse 25 — The High Priest's Death as Release:**
The manslayer's confinement ends only at the death of the high priest (הַכֹּהֵן הַגָּדֹל, *hakkohen haggadol*) who was anointed with the holy oil. This is theologically profound: the high priest's death functions as a kind of atonement or epoch-marker, releasing accumulated bloodguilt. The life of the high priest — anointed, set apart — bears a representative weight for the whole community. His death resets the moral ledger of the land.

**Verse 30 — Witness Requirements:**
The plural עֵדִים (*'edim*, "witnesses") establishes the principle that capital punishment requires corroboration. A single witness (עֵד אֶחָד, *'ed 'echad*) is explicitly insufficient — a safeguard against false accusation and judicial murder. This principle echoes through Deuteronomy 17:6 and 19:15 and into later Jewish and Christian legal thought.

**Verses 31-32 — No Ransom (כֹּפֶר):**
The word כֹּפֶר (*kofer*) means "ransom" or "covering payment" — from the same root as כִּפֻּרִים (*kippurim*, "atonement"). The law prohibits monetary substitution both for the convicted murderer and for the manslayer who wishes to leave his city of refuge early. Human life is beyond price. This stands in deliberate contrast to other ancient Near Eastern legal codes (such as the Laws of Eshnunna) that permitted monetary compensation for homicide.

**Verses 33-34 — Land Pollution and Divine Presence:**
The Hebrew uses two different verbs: חָנַף (*chanaf*, "to pollute/profane") in verse 33 and טָמֵא (*tame'*, "to make unclean/defile") in verse 34. Blood shed unjustly does not merely offend human sensibility — it corrupts the very soil. The chapter concludes with the theological foundation for all its legislation: YHWH dwells (שֹׁכֵן, *shokhen*) in the midst of the sons of Israel. The divine presence within the land demands that the land itself be kept from defilement. Unpunished murder makes the land uninhabitable for God. This theology of sacred space undergirds the entire system of cities of refuge, blood-avenger protocols, and the prohibition on ransom.
